text: The surgeon marks the proximal margin on the sigmoid colon, noting the absence of diverticula in the soft, pliable bowel. Evidence of previous inflammatory disease is visible in the pelvis.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, carefully moving tissues towards the midline to expose the ureter.
The surgeon navigates over the left kidney towards the splenic flexure, visible just beyond the ridge in the surgical field.
The surgeon assesses the bowel length to ensure a tension-free anastomosis, then proceeds to dissect and take the mesentery attached to the specimen while preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon creates a mesenteric window by carefully dissecting through the thickened, woody mesentery, taking multiple bites to progress through the dense tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection, working down towards the upper rectum while preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon examines and cleans the planned surgical margin, confirming the blood supply before proceeding to transect the margin using scissors for a transrectal specimen extraction.

The surgeon divides the rectosigmoid colon and morselates the specimen into smaller pieces, using a vessel sealer to prevent blood oozing from the trapped vessels within the specimen. Each smaller piece is extracted sequentially.
After cleaning the surgical area, the surgeon begins sewing an end-to-end anastomosis, starting at the posterior midline of the bowel.

The surgeon sews the end-to-end anastomosis, starting on the patient's left side, taking full thickness bites of the posterior bowel wall. The uterus is resuspended for better visualization, and the surgeon continues around the left lateral aspect of the anastomosis, ensuring healthy bowel edges. Interrupted trio silk sutures are used in areas requiring reinforcement.

The surgeon turns to begin suturing over the anterior row of the anastomosis. They start the second suture in the posterior midline, adjacent to the first, and proceed to suture towards the patient's right.

The surgeon aligns the mucosal layers of the bowel for the anastomosis, ensuring a precise fit. The open lumens of the bowel are checked for perfusion to confirm satisfactory blood flow, with adjustments made to the anastomosis as needed.
The surgeon begins the anterior canal suture, taking it across to the original stitch on the left side, then brings the suture back towards the midline to avoid tying knots in the corners.

The surgeon closes the right lower quadrant trocar site using a suture passer, as this site is typically below the arcuate line and at risk for hernia formation. The specimen has already been extracted, eliminating the need for an additional incision.
The surgeon cuts the original suture and ties two ends together, then oversows the area with a 3-0 silk suture to reinforce the knot.
The surgeon performs a leak test by driving the scope through the anastomosis, observing the area for bubbles to ensure the integrity of the surgical connection.
The surgeon places a wad of omentum into the pelvis to cover the anastomosis site.
